mnemonics.title: Address-of Operator
mnemonics.content: & (ampersand) = 'AND' = Ask for the ADDRESS of the variable. Think: '& gives the location'.

mnemonics.title: Dereference Operator
mnemonics.content: * (asterisk) = 'STAR' = STARS point to something far away. Use * to go TO that location and GET the value. Think: '* goes to the address and FETCHES the value'.

mnemonics.title: Arrow Operator
mnemonics.content: -> = 'Goes to member'. Use with pointers to structures. Alternative: (*ptr).member is longer, so use -> for 'arrow-way' shortcut.

acronyms.usage: int *p = &x; gets address of variable x

acronyms.expansion: Dereference/Indirection operator - accesses value at address
acronyms.usage: int y = *p; gets value stored at address in p

acronyms.expansion: Arrow operator - member access through pointer
acronyms.usage: ptr->member accesses structure member via pointer
int *p = NULL; initializes pointer to invalid address
Can be cast to any pointer type for flexibility

visualTips.concept: Pointer as a house address sign
visualTips.visualization: Think of a pointer as a street sign showing where a 'house' (variable) is located. & gives you the sign with the address, * goes to that house and tells you who lives there.
Imagine an array as a row of houses. ptr points to house 0. ptr+1 jumps to the next house (not the next door number). The 'jump distance' equals sizeof(type) houses.
Think of a pointer to pointer as a business card that has another business card on it. You need to follow two addresses to reach the final information.

analogies.analogy: A pointer is like a mailing address written on an envelope, and the variable is the house at that address. & tells you what's written on the envelope, * tells you what's inside the house.
The array name is like a hotel directory that points to room 1. The first room is where the directory naturally points.
NULL is like writing 'No fixed address' or 'Address unknown' on an envelope - it points to nothing valid.

analogies.analization: Incrementing a pointer is like moving to the next house on the street. How far you move depends on the 'type' of house - a mansion (double) takes more steps than a hut (char).
